steps.text: Train each employee involved in operating a process (or before being involved in a newly assigned process) in an overview of the process and operating procedures.
Certify in writing that the employee has the required knowledge, skills, and abilities to safely carry out duties.
Determine the appropriate frequency of refresher training in consultation with the employees.
Provide refresher training at least every three years, and more often if necessary.
Ascertain that each employee involved in operating a process has received and understood the training.
Prepare a record containing the identity of the employee, the date of training, and the means used to verify understanding.
Assure that each contract employee is trained in the work practices necessary to perform his/her job.

preconditions.text: Employee was already involved in operating a process on May 26, 1992.
Employee is about to be involved in operating a newly assigned process.

constraints.text: The training shall include emphasis on the specific safety and health hazards, emergency operations including shutdown, and safe work practices.
Monitoring compliance must be performed by reliable persons properly instructed and given authority.

steps.text: Provide training consisting of on-the-job training, classroom-type training, or a combination of both, covering required subjects (recognition of dangers, emergency procedures, and first-aid).
Prepare a certification record including the identity of the person trained, the signature of the employer or trainer, and the completion date.
Maintain the certification record on file for the duration of the employee's employment.
Make the certification record available upon request to the Assistant Secretary for Occupational Safety and Health.

preconditions.text: Employees must not engage in activities until they have received proper training, unless the employer can demonstrate the employee was already trained prior to employment.
Only persons trained in the operation of the derrick shall be permitted to operate the derrick.

constraints.text: The certification record must be prepared at the completion of training.

steps.text: Inform employees of the content of Appendices A and B of the lead regulation.
Institute a lead training program and ensure employee participation.
Provide initial lead training prior to initial job assignment (or within 180 days of the effective date).
Repeat the lead training program at least annually.
Inform each employee of the standard content, specific exposure operations, and respirator details.
Inform employee of exposure over the permissible exposure limit for inorganic arsenic and the corrective actions being taken.

preconditions.text: Employee is exposed over the permissible exposure limit for inorganic arsenic.
Workplace has potential exposure to airborne lead at any level.
Employee is subject to lead exposure at or above the action level, or skin/eye irritation possibility exists.

constraints.text: Exposure to airborne lead hazard
Exposure to inorganic arsenic hazard

steps.text: Provide to the Assistant Secretary or the Director all materials relating to the employee information and the training program.
Provide employees with benzene information and training.
Provide employees with benzene information and training at least annually.
Provide employees with an explanation of the contents of this section, including Appendices A and B, and indicate where the standard is available.
Describe the medical surveillance program and explain the information contained in Appendix C.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation for coke oven emissions.
Provide the coke oven emissions training program at the time of initial assignment (or as of January 27, 1977 for employees employed at that time).
Provide the coke oven emissions training program at least annually.

preconditions.text: Upon request by the Assistant Secretary or the Director.
At the time of initial assignment to a work area where benzene is present.
If exposures are above the action level.
For employees who are employed in a regulated area.

constraints.text: The training program shall be in accordance with the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.1200(h)(1) and (2), and shall include specific information on benzene for each category of information included in that section.

steps.text: Provide to the Director, upon request, all available materials relating to employee information and training.
Make all employees whose work may necessitate their presence in an area covered by the signal familiar with the actual sound of the signal.
Make all employees normally working in the area acquainted with the signal by actual demonstration at their work locations.
Inform all individuals working in or frequenting any portion of a radiation area of the occurrence of radioactive materials or of radiation in such portions.
Instruct individuals in the safety problems associated with exposure to radioactive materials or radiation and in precautions or devices to minimize exposure.
Instruct individuals in the applicable provisions of this section for the protection of employees from exposure to radiation or radioactive materials.
Advise individuals of reports of radiation exposure which employees must request pursuant to the regulations.
Provide employees with effective information and training on hazardous chemicals in their work area.
Inform employees of the requirements of this section, and any operations in their work area where hazardous chemicals are present.

preconditions.text: Before placing the system into operation
At the time of their initial assignment
Whenever a new chemical hazard the employees have not previously been trained about is introduced into their work area

constraints.text: Chemical-specific information must always be available through labels and material safety data sheets.

steps.text: Visually inspect and test each space certified as 'Safe for Workers' or 'Safe for Hot Work' as often as necessary.

preconditions.text: A Marine Chemist's or Coast Guard authorized person's certificate has been issued.

constraints.text: Ensure that atmospheric conditions within that space are maintained within the conditions established by the certificate.
The visual inspection and testing must be performed by a competent person.

steps.text: Initiate and maintain accident prevention programs as may be necessary to comply with this part.
Provide for frequent and regular inspections of the job sites, materials, and equipment to be made by competent persons designated by the employers.
Permit only those employees qualified by training or experience to operate equipment and machinery.
Train each affected employee in the manner required by the standard.

preconditions.text: Employee must be qualified by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or professional standing, or by extensive knowledge, training, and experience, successfully demonstrating their ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the subject matter, the work, or the project.

constraints.text: Inspections must be conducted by competent persons capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who have authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.
Each failure to train an employee may be considered a separate violation.

steps.text: Determine the appropriate frequency of refresher training in consultation with the employees involved in operating the process.
Provide refresher training to each employee involved in operating a process to assure understanding and adherence to current operating procedures.
Ascertain that each employee involved in operating a process has received and understood the required training.
Prepare a training record containing the identity of the employee, the date of training, and the means used to verify understanding.
Assure that each contract employee is trained in the work practices necessary to safely perform his/her job.
Assure that each contract employee is instructed in the known potential hazards related to his/her job and the process, and the applicable provisions of the emergency action plan.
Document that each contract employee has received and understood the training by preparing a record with employee identity, date of training, and verification means.
Confirm during the pre-startup safety review that training of each employee involved in operating a process has been completed.
Train each employee involved in maintaining the on-going integrity of process equipment in an overview of that process, its hazards, and applicable job task procedures.

preconditions.text: Prior to the introduction of highly hazardous chemicals to a process

constraints.text: Refresher training must be provided at least every three years, and more often if necessary.
Instruction must cover known potential fire, explosion, or toxic release hazards.

steps.text: Provide appropriate training to maintenance personnel to ensure they understand preventive maintenance program procedures, safe practices, and the proper use of special equipment.
Develop a form or clearance sheet to facilitate the processing of changes through management of change procedures.
Investigate incidents and near misses that resulted in or could reasonably have resulted in a catastrophic release.

constraints.text: The change form should include description, technical basis, safety and health considerations, documentation of changes, and approvals.
Incident investigation must identify underlying causes and implement steps to prevent similar events from occurring.

steps.text: Maintain training records, including courses presented, names of attenders, successful completions, and certificates issued.
Provide training records upon student's request or as mandated by law.
Conduct or direct an annual written audit of the training program.
Document, approve, and implement program modifications to address deficiencies.
Store and maintain the audit and program modification documents at the training facility.

preconditions.text: Deficiencies are identified during the annual written audit.

constraints.text: Maintain training records for a minimum of five years after the date an individual participated in the training program.
Audit and program modification documents must be maintained specifically at the training facility.

steps.text: Familiarize with 29 CFR 1926.60.

Review the principles and practices for analyzing an incident to determine the hazardous substances present, their physical and chemical properties, the likely behavior of the hazardous substance and its container, the types of hazardous substance transportation containers and vehicles involved in the release, the appropriate strategy for approaching release sites and containing the release.

Review procedures for implementing continuing response actions consistent with the local emergency response plan, the organization’s standard operating procedures, and the current edition of DOT’s ERG including extended emergency notification procedures and follow-up communications.
Review the principles and practice for proper selection and use of personal protective equipment.
Review the principles and practices of establishing exposure zones, proper decontamination and medical surveillance stations and procedures.
Review the expected hazards including fire and explosions hazards, confined space hazards, electrical hazards, powered equipment hazards, motor vehicle hazards, and walking-working surface hazards.

Obtain awareness and knowledge of the competencies for the Hazardous Materials Technician covered in the National Fire Protection Association’s Standard No. 472, Professional Competence of Responders to Hazardous Materials Incidents.
Review and demonstrate competency in performing the applicable skills of 29 CFR 1926.65(q).
Gain hands-on experience with retrieval and use of written and electronic information.
Train each employee who performs work while on a scaffold by a person qualified in the subject matter to recognize hazards and understand control procedures.
Instruct the employee on the nature of any electrical hazards, fall hazards, and falling object hazards in the work area.
Instruct the employee on the correct procedures for dealing with electrical hazards and for erecting, maintaining, and disassembling the fall protection and falling object protection systems.
Instruct the employee on the proper use of the scaffold and the proper handling of materials on the scaffold.
Instruct the employee on the maximum intended load and the load-carrying capacities of the scaffolds used.
Instruct the employee on any other pertinent requirements of this subpart.

preconditions.text: Employee is assigned to perform work while on a scaffold

constraints.text: Scaffolds shall be erected, moved, dismantled, or altered only under the supervision and direction of a competent person qualified in scaffold erection, moving, dismantling or alteration.
Scaffold erection, moving, dismantling, or alteration activities shall be performed only by experienced and trained employees selected for such work by the competent person.

steps.text: Institute a training program for employees potentially exposed to cadmium.
Provide training to each employee potentially exposed to cadmium.
Ensure employee participation in the training program.
Maintain a record of the contents of the training program.

Make the training program understandable to the employee and inform them of the required topics (A through H), including health hazards, cadmium details, engineering controls, protective measures, respirators, medical surveillance, section contents, and access rights.
Make a copy of this section and its materials available.

preconditions.text: Prior to or at the time of initial assignment to a job involving potential exposure to cadmium.

constraints.text: Training must be provided at least annually after the initial assignment.
The training program must be understandable to the employee.

steps.text: Demonstrate that the signal person meets the qualification requirements through an oral or written test, and through a practical test.
Train each employee specified in 1926.1408(g) and 1926.1410(m) in overhead power line topics.
Train each employee assigned to work as a signal person who does not meet the requirements of 1926.1428(c).
Train each operator-in-training in the areas addressed in 1926.1427(j).
Provide re-training if the operator-in-training does not pass a qualification or certification test.
Train each operator who has not yet been certified or qualified in the areas addressed in 1926.1427(j) during the transitional period.
Train each operator excepted under 1926.1427(a) on the safe operation of the equipment the operator will be using.
Train each operator of the equipment covered.

preconditions.text: Signal person knows and understands the type of signals used (including the Standard Method for hand signals if used).
Signal person is competent in the application of the type of signals used.
Signal person has a basic understanding of equipment operation and limitations, including crane dynamics.
Signal person knows and understands the relevant requirements of 1926.1419 through 1926.1422 and 1926.1428.
Employee is assigned to work as a signal person and does not meet the requirements of 1926.1428(c).
The operator-in-training does not pass a qualification or certification test.
During the four-year phase-in period for operator certification or qualification, and the operator has not yet been certified or qualified.
Operator is excepted under 1926.1427(a) from the requirements of 1926.1427.
